  • Analytics API Reference

Analytics API Reference#

Performance metrics module (Viz Ref §1.1–1.6 + PLAN §5.7, 5.8, 5.18).

Implements the complete metric suite required by the CW2 report:

  • Return & risk-adjusted (Sharpe, Sortino, IR, Calmar, annualised vol)

  • Drawdown & tail (Max DD + duration, 99% HVaR, 99% ES, skew, kurt)

  • Distribution (hit-rate, best/worst month, % negative, downside dev)

  • Long/short-specific (leg alphas, gross/net exposure, turnover, β)

  • Factor diagnostics (per-factor IC, IC-IR, % positive IC months, FM-Betas)

  • Headline 4×17 exhibit (§1.6 template — fills the Section 4.1 table)

  • Block bootstrap CI (Politis-Romano 1994, 6-month stationary block)

  • Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Bailey & López de Prado 2014)

  • Probabilistic Sharpe (PSR at threshold)

  • Minimum Backtest Length (§5.18, Bailey et al. 2017)

analytics.performance.annualised_return(monthly_returns: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.annualised_turnover(turnover_series: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.annualised_volatility(monthly_returns: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.best_month(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.calmar_ratio(returns: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.circular_block_bootstrap_sharpe(returns: Series, block_size: int = 6, n_bootstrap: int = 1000, seed: int = 42, ann: int = 12) → dict[source]#

Politis-Romano (1994) circular block bootstrap of Sharpe ratio.

Returns dict with mean, 5%, 95% Sharpe CI.

analytics.performance.compute_headline_metrics(returns_df: DataFrame, rf_series: Series | float = 0.0, benchmark_col: str = 'benchmark_ew', ann: int = 12) → DataFrame[source]#

Produce the Section-4.1 headline exhibit (Viz Ref §1.6 template).

Columns: Dynamic Gross · Dynamic Net 20bp · Static Net 20bp · Benchmark EW

analytics.performance.deflated_sharpe_ratio(observed_sharpe: float, n_trials: int, returns: Series, ann: int = 12) → dict[source]#

Bailey & López de Prado (2014).

DSR = Φ(z) where z accounts for sample length, skew, kurtosis and the multiplicity of trials tested.

analytics.performance.downside_deviation(returns: Series, mar: float = 0.0, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.drawdown_duration_months(returns: Series) → int[source]#
analytics.performance.drawdown_series(returns: Series) → Series[source]#
analytics.performance.excess_kurtosis(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.expected_shortfall(returns: Series, confidence: float = 0.99) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.factor_ic_ir(ic_series: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.gross_exposure(weights: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.historical_var(returns: Series, confidence: float = 0.99) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.information_ratio(returns: Series, benchmark: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.max_drawdown(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.minimum_backtest_length(target_sharpe: float = 1.0, n_trials: int = 15, alpha: float = 0.05, gamma_const: float = 0.5772) → float[source]#

Bailey, Borwein, LdP, Zhu (2017) — minimum track record length.

Returns the minimum number of months required to reject H0 at (1-α) confidence for a given target annualised Sharpe, accounting for the multiplicity of trials.

analytics.performance.monthly_hit_rate(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.net_exposure(weights: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.pct_negative_months(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.pct_positive_ic_months(ic_series: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.portfolio_beta(portfolio_returns: Series, benchmark: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.probabilistic_sharpe_ratio(observed_sharpe: float, threshold_sharpe: float, returns: Series, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.sharpe_ratio(returns: Series, rf_series: Series | float = 0.0, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#

Annualised Sharpe ratio with numerical-pathology guards.

Second-pass audit finding: previous implementation guarded only σ, not μ. Now returns 0.0 for (μ or σ) in {±inf, NaN}. Prevents bad CW1 data from propagating an infinite Sharpe into the headline exhibit.

analytics.performance.skewness(returns: Series) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.sortino_ratio(returns: Series, rf_series=0.0, ann: int = 12) → float[source]#
analytics.performance.worst_month(returns: Series) → float[source]#

Validation checks on engine outputs (PLAN §3.1 specialist scope — Peixi).

Consumes the seven Parquet data-contract files and verifies the internal consistency invariants listed in the Task Allocation Guide §3.1.

class analytics.validation.ValidationResult(passed: 'bool', flags: 'list[str]', details: 'dict[str, Any]')[source]#

Bases: object

passed: bool#
flags: list[str]#
details: dict[str, Any]#
__init__(passed: bool, flags: list[str], details: dict[str, Any]) → None#
analytics.validation.validate_factors(factor_df: DataFrame, tol: float = 0.5) → ValidationResult[source]#

Z-scores should have mean ~0 (within tolerance) and std ~1 per factor × date × sector.

analytics.validation.validate_regime(regime_df: DataFrame) → ValidationResult[source]#
analytics.validation.validate_returns(returns_df: DataFrame) → ValidationResult[source]#
analytics.validation.validate_weights(weights_df: DataFrame, max_weight: float = 0.05) → ValidationResult[source]#

Per-date, per-leg: w ≥ 0 (after leg sign), sum = ±0.5 each leg, no w > 5%, gross ~2, net ~0.

Charts module — all 14 mandated visualisations plus 3 extensions.

Strictly follows the Viz Reference Part 2 Visual Style Guide:

Navy #1B2A4A (dynamic) · Blue #2E75B6 (static/comparison) · Grey #7F8C8D (benchmark, dotted) · Red #C0392B (drawdown, losses) · Green #27AE60 (positive). Factor colours: Momentum=Navy, Value=Blue, Quality=Green, Sentiment=Orange. Figure sizes: 12×5.5 (time-series), 7×5.5 (heatmaps), 8×5 (bar charts). 150 DPI on-screen, 300 DPI for submission. Dates: “MMM ‘YY”; Percent axes via PercentFormatter.

analytics.charts.plot_ablation(ablation_df: DataFrame, metric: str = 'sharpe_net') → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_bandit_posterior(bandit_log_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

Extension 17: Arm posterior-mean trajectory over time (TS learning).

analytics.charts.plot_cost_comparison(returns_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_covariance(cov_matrix: DataFrame, title: str = 'Ledoit-Wolf Shrunk Covariance') → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_covid_zoom(returns_df: DataFrame, regime_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_cumulative_return(returns_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

Three-line plot: dynamic (navy, filled) / static (blue dashed) / benchmark (grey dotted). Growth-of-$1; horizontal reference at 1.0.

analytics.charts.plot_deflated_sharpe_distribution(bootstrap_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

Extension 15: bootstrap Sharpe distribution with 95% CI band.

analytics.charts.plot_drawdown(dd_series: Series) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_factor_attribution(contrib_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

contrib_df: columns year, factor, contribution.

analytics.charts.plot_ff5_regression_loadings(regression_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

Extension 16: FF5+Mom regression β with Newey-West 95% CI bars.

analytics.charts.plot_ls_decomposition(exposure_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_param_sensitivity(grid_df: DataFrame, metric: str = 'sharpe_net') → Figure[source]#

Heatmap with annotated cells and bordered optimal cell.

analytics.charts.plot_rolling_ic(ic_df: DataFrame, window: int = 3) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_rolling_sharpe(returns_df: DataFrame, window: int = 12) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_sector_exposure(weights_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_turnover(exposure_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#
analytics.charts.plot_vix_regime_returns(returns_df: DataFrame, regime_df: DataFrame) → Figure[source]#

Parameter-sensitivity grid search with Combinatorial Purged CV (§5.5).

Runs γ × λ grid search, evaluating every combination on CPCV folds with:

  • 12 disjoint month-groups

  • 2-month purge + 1-month embargo

  • joblib parallel execution

Output: sensitivity_grid.parquet (one row per (γ, λ, fold)).

analytics.sensitivity.run_sensitivity_cpcv(cfg: Config, start: date, end: date, out_dir: str | Path = 'output') → DataFrame[source]#

Run full γ × λ grid with CPCV and deflated-Sharpe evaluation.

Due to the number of runs (gamma×lambda×folds), we use a light variant: for each (γ, λ) we run ONE full backtest over [start, end], then split the resulting monthly returns into CPCV folds — much faster than re-running the engine per fold, and statistically equivalent as long as dynamics are effectively stationary within each month.

Factor-ablation analysis (PLAN §5.13 / Viz Ref chart 13).

Five backtest variants:

  1. full_4factor — baseline dynamic strategy

  2. no_momentum — momentum weight = 0, others renormalised

  3. no_value — value weight = 0

  4. no_quality — quality weight = 0

  5. no_sentiment — sentiment weight = 0

Output: ablation_results.parquet.

analytics.ablation.run_ablation(cfg: Config, start: date, end: date, out_dir: str | Path = 'output') → DataFrame[source]#

Run 5 ablation variants; return a one-row-per-variant DataFrame.

Static vs dynamic head-to-head comparison (PLAN §10.3).

Four variants:

  1. Static baseline (cfg.factors.base_weights — 50/50 mom + val)

  2. VIX-only tilting (γ=0, λ as tuned)

  3. Dispersion-only scaling (γ as tuned, λ=0)

  4. Combined (full dynamic model)

Output: comparison_results.parquet.

analytics.comparison.run_static_vs_dynamic(cfg: Config, start: date, end: date, out_dir: str | Path = 'output') → DataFrame[source]#

Stress testing — COVID, 2022 rate shock, Q4 2025 reversal (PLAN §10.4).

Plus Monte Carlo permutation test for dynamic-vs-static Sharpe gap (§5.13).

Output: stress_results.parquet.

analytics.stress.permutation_test_dynamic_vs_static(returns_df: DataFrame, n_permutations: int = 10000, seed: int = 42) → dict[source]#

Null: dynamic and static draw from same distribution.

Under H0, permute the dynamic/static labels across months, recompute the Sharpe difference. Return empirical p-value.

analytics.stress.run_stress(cfg: Config, out_dir: str | Path = 'output') → DataFrame[source]#

Attribution analysis — FF5+Mom regression + Brinson-Fachler + IC stats.

Consumes engine outputs (factor_premia, factor_ic, returns) to produce the empirical-section attribution tables & numbers.

Fama-French + Momentum alpha regression#

For each strategy variant, regress monthly strategy returns on Mkt-RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA, MOM with Newey-West HAC standard errors.

Synthetic FF factors#

We don’t fetch the real Kenneth French data in this prototype; instead we synthesise reasonable FF approximations from the CW1 universe (cap-weighted benchmark as Mkt, long-short B/P decile portfolios as HML, etc.). Real FF factors should be plugged in for the final report — hook is clean.

analytics.attribution_analysis.brinson_fachler(weights_df: DataFrame, returns_df: DataFrame, gics_map: dict[str, str]) → DataFrame[source]#

Brinson-Fachler (1985) allocation vs selection decomposition.

Allocation effect = (w_port_sector - w_bench_sector) × (r_bench_sector - r_bench_total) Selection effect = w_bench_sector × (r_port_sector - r_bench_sector)

analytics.attribution_analysis.compute_ic_statistics(ic_df: DataFrame) → DataFrame[source]#
analytics.attribution_analysis.run_ff5_mom_regression(strategy_returns: Series, ff_factors: DataFrame, nw_lags: int = 4) → DataFrame[source]#

OLS with Newey-West HAC SEs.

Parameters:

  • strategy_returns (Series (index=date))

  • ff_factors (DataFrame with columns Mkt-RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA, MOM (optional subset))

  • nw_lags (int) – Newey-West lag per Andrews (1991).

Return type:

DataFrame with columns factor, beta, se_nw, t_stat, p_value

analytics.attribution_analysis.synthesize_ff_factors(returns_df: DataFrame, benchmark_col: str = 'benchmark_ew') → DataFrame[source]#

Rough FF proxies built from available columns.

This is a placeholder — for the final report, the real Kenneth-French dataset should be downloaded (via pandas-datareader or CSV). For now, we produce enough columns to exercise the regression pipeline.
